hypotheses: Hypothesis 1: Products predominantly associated with utilitarian attitudes at the category level support less utilitarian and more symbolic attitudes at the brand level.
Hypothesis 2: Products predominantly associated with symbolic attitudes at the category level support less symbolic and more utilitarian attitudes at the brand level.
claim.hypotheses_location: Section 'The Effect of Branding on Attitude Functions'
claim.statement: Branding alters the associations between products and attitude functions, affecting the persuasiveness of utilitarian and symbolic appeals.

data.sample_size: Study 1A: 132, Study 1B: 206, Study 1C: 139, Study 2A: 117, Study 2B: 72, Study 3: 54, Study 4: 102
data.notes: Studies tested various brands and categories; branding changes perceptions of attitude functions.

method.description: The study conducted experiments to test how branding affects the attitude functions of products, using different products and brands to measure the change in persuasiveness of utilitarian versus symbolic appeals.
steps: Participants were assigned different products, either branded or unbranded.
They completed surveys measuring their attitudes towards these products.
Various statistical analyses such as ANOVA were conducted to evaluate the differences in attitudes and the effectiveness of persuasive appeals.
method.independent_variables: Branding status (branded or unbranded), type of appeal (utilitarian or symbolic)

results.summary: The study found that attitudes towards products change with branding, where utilitarian product categories become more symbolic with branding, and symbolic product categories become more utilitarian. The efficacy of matching appeals diminishes at the brand level.

metadata.original_paper_title: Branding Alters Attitude Functions and Reduces the Advantage of Function-Matching Persuasive Appeals
